title: Research
text: I connect genetic variation to the ecological and evolutionary processes that produce it. I work across scales, from the DNA within a single coral colony to patterns of diversity across many species and reefs, using genomics and molecular tools to understand how marine life evolves and to inform its conservation.

title: Research
text:
Coral population & conservation genomics
This is the core of my PhD thesis. I use population and evolutionary genomics to uncover hidden diversity in reef corals and to understand the processes that generate and maintain it, including speciation, gene flow, reproductive isolation and local adaptation.
My thesis centres on the brooding coral Seriatopora hystrix along the Great Barrier Reef, where genomic data reveal cryptic species and habitat specialisation: lineages that look alike but are genetically distinct and sorted across reef environments. Reading these patterns through the lens of gene flow and divergence helps clarify how coral species form and persist, and how much of what we treat as a single species is in fact several.
Alongside this species-level work I take a macrogenetic view, synthesising and comparing genetic diversity across many species and across space to ask what general rules shape diversity on reefs. Related projects include estimates of genetic diversity and differentiation in the Caribbean coral Agaricia tenuifolia, and causal modelling showing that corals in more environmentally heterogeneous reefs can carry lower genetic diversity. Together this work supports efforts such as the Reef Restoration and Adaptation Program to conserve and restore corals in a warming ocean.




Seriatopora hystrix colony in the field





Close-up of Seriatopora hystrix branches





Branching Acropora coral

title: Research
text:
Environmental DNA & biodiversity monitoring
Traces of DNA shed into seawater can reveal the species present on a reef without ever seeing them. I use environmental DNA (eDNA) and DNA metabarcoding to detect and monitor biodiversity, approaches that can make surveys faster, less invasive and more sensitive to hard-to-observe life stages. My Honours research developed metabarcoding to characterise the spatio-temporal distribution of planktonic echinoderm larvae, and I have since applied eDNA to monitor benthic communities in Quandamooka (Moreton Bay).
I have also contributed to reef community monitoring through Reef Check Australia surveys and the Mooloolaba Ecological Assessment and Mapping project.

title: Research
text:
Beyond marine systems
While my work so far has focused on marine systems, the questions that drive me are not confined to the ocean. The genomic and analytical approaches I use apply broadly across taxa, and I am always keen to extend them to new systems, questions and collaborations.






For a full list of talks and posters, see Publications & Talks. My published work is listed on my research profiles.

title: Ilha Byrne
text:
Ilha Byrne
    Zoologist · Evolutionary biologist · Science communicator
    
      
      
      
      
    
  



  About
  Research
  Publications & Talks
  Teaching & Outreach
  Photography
  CV & Contact

title: About
text:
I am a zoologist and PhD candidate in ecology and evolutionary biology at the University of Queensland in Brisbane, Australia. My research asks how genetic diversity and adaptation are shaped in marine invertebrates, particularly reef-building corals, and what that means for their conservation in a changing ocean.
My path into science began underwater. Before university I trained and worked as a PADI Divemaster, which turned a love of the ocean into a determination to understand and protect it. I went on to study at UQ, completing First-Class Honours on using DNA metabarcoding to characterise the distribution of planktonic echinoderm larvae, and graduating with the University Medal as Faculty Graduate of the Year.
Since then I have worked across molecular labs, reef fieldwork and coastal communities, with the Riginos Research Group, the Marine Spatial Ecology Lab and the Tibbetts Lab, and on programs including the Reef Restoration and Adaptation Program. My work combines population and conservation genomics, evolutionary genomics and environmental DNA to connect patterns of genetic variation to the environments and evolutionary processes that produce them.
I am supported by an ELEVATE Boosting Women in STEM Scholarship and was a Global Change Scholar with UQ and CSIRO. Beyond the lab and the reef, I am an active science communicator and a long-standing volunteer with Reef Check Australia, working to bring science to schools, community groups and the public.




Fieldwork on the water





Reef flat at Heron Island, Great Barrier Reef

title: Teaching & Outreach
text: Sharing marine science with students, communities and the public is central to how I work. Alongside my research I teach at university, communicate science online and in classrooms, and volunteer for reef conservation.

title: Teaching & Outreach
text:
Teaching
I have tutored and demonstrated undergraduate courses at the University of Queensland since 2018, and teach into international study-abroad programs.

International Programs Tutor, University of Queensland: University of California and Stanford University Study Abroad Programs.
Demonstrator: Environment & Society (GEOS1100), Zoology (BIOL2204) and Marine Science (MARS1001).
Peer-Assisted Study Session & Science Learning Centre Tutor: university-funded academic tutoring and mentoring in biology, genetics, chemistry and statistics.

title: Teaching & Outreach
text:
Science communication & outreach

Science communication: I share marine science and life as a coral researcher on Instagram @biologist.byrne.
Wonder of Science: led and supported STEM activities for school students across Queensland, including in regional and remote communities (an initiative of the Queensland Chief Scientist).
Curious Minds Coach: mentored high-school students who are aspiring women in STEM.
Reef Check Australia Ambassador: represented reef science and conservation at markets, festivals and community events, and coordinated the Coast to Coral online seminar series.
Science engagement: developed and delivered in-classroom and outdoor STEM experiences for high-school students, and university STEM and recruitment events.

title: Teaching & Outreach
text:

HDR Chair, Centre for Marine Science Committee, UQ.

Organising committee, UQ–CNRS West Pacific Marine Biology Symposium.
